contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Re-evaluate binary search versus scanning whenever query semantics, preprocessing reuse, mutation rate, access model, predicate monotonicity, or output contract changes.

items.prompt: Scenario A: one exact query on unsorted data. Scenario B: the same data is now maintained in sorted random-access form and receives many exact queries. How should the strategy transfer?

options.text: Use a scan in A; use binary search in B after verifying the maintained order and query key.
Use binary search in both because the task is called search.
Use scanning in both because it was correct in A.
Sort from scratch before every query in both scenarios.

feedback.reason: Strategy should change when the data representation and workload cross the transfer boundary.
feedback.details: The underlying query is similar, but legality and total cost differ between one unsorted query and many aligned sorted queries.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_both: A has no legal half-discard rule.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_both: B adds stable ordered structure and repeated workload that favor binary search.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_each: A does not amortize sorting and B is already maintained sorted.

items.prompt: A sorted array initially asks for any exact target. The requirement changes to the first record in original insertion order among matching keys, and no provenance is stored. What changes?

options.text: The key-only binary search no longer satisfies the output contract; scan the original order or build a provenance-aware index.
Keep returning any midpoint match.
Return the leftmost sorted duplicate and assume it is earliest inserted.
The query is impossible under every representation.

feedback.reason: A change in output identity can invalidate an otherwise legal search strategy.
feedback.details: Binary search transfers again if the ordered index is enriched with the required original-position metadata.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.leftmost_sorted: Sorted duplicate order need not preserve insertion order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_solution: Scanning the original or storing provenance can answer it.

items.prompt: A predicate-search problem changes from “capacity at least x is feasible” to “capacity x is feasible only when x is even.” What strategy change is required?

options.text: The predicate is no longer monotonic; scan candidates or reformulate with additional structure.
Keep the same first-true binary search.
Change only the midpoint bias.
Sort candidate capacities.

feedback.reason: A small semantic change can remove the one-transition property.
feedback.details: The decision must be re-derived from the new predicate, not inherited from the previous problem label.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_capacity: They are already ordered; the truth pattern remains arbitrary.

items.prompt: An arbitrary-condition scan becomes performance-critical. A maintained sorted index of exactly the matching records is introduced. Which transfer is now possible?

options.text: Search the new query-aligned index with binary search for supported ordered queries.
Binary-search the original arbitrary condition without using the index.
The new index can never change the algorithm.
Use the index without accounting for update maintenance.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing can create a new ordered domain where none existed before.
feedback.details: The transfer boundary includes whether index construction and maintenance are legal and worthwhile.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_index_only: The representation now encodes the missing structure.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_maintenance: Correctness and total cost depend on freshness and maintenance.

items.prompt: A stable sorted array receives many queries, but each query asks whether any value satisfies a different arbitrary callback. Which default remains appropriate?

options.text: Scan for each callback unless the callback contract guarantees monotonicity or a matching index exists.
Binary-search every callback because the data is sorted.
Cache one midpoint and use it for every callback.
Sort again before each callback.

feedback.reason: Repeated-query volume does not make an illegal predicate binary-searchable.
feedback.details: Volume may justify building additional indexes, but correctness structure still comes first.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_all_callbacks: The callbacks may not align with the sort order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.reuse_mid: Different arbitrary predicates can have unrelated matches.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_again: The sort key remains unrelated to callback truth.

items.prompt: A one-query unsorted workload grows into q queries, but the data also changes before every query. Which conclusion is safest?

options.text: Recompute preprocessing and maintenance costs; high q alone does not guarantee sorting once is useful.
Switch automatically to binary search because q is large.
Never reconsider the scan because it was initially correct.
Evaluate only query cost.

feedback.reason: Transfer requires all changed constraints to be included, not just query count.
feedback.details: The boundary is determined by the ratio of query savings to index maintenance cost.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_due_q: Frequent changes may invalidate or rebuild the order each time.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_forever: Incremental indexes or changed update/query ratios may later pay off.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_updates: Updates are part of the workload.

items.prompt: A sorted array exact-search implementation is moved to a sequential-only storage API. What should happen to the strategy analysis?

options.text: Reassess midpoint-access cost; a scan or a separate random-access index may become preferable.
Keep claiming O(log n) because the values remain sorted.
Sort the API endpoints.
Rename the sequential cursor to mid.

feedback.reason: Algorithm transfer across data structures must preserve the operation-cost model.
feedback.details: The same abstract order can support different practical and asymptotic lookup costs.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_api: The issue is random access, not order alone.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rename_cursor: Names do not change access complexity.

items.prompt: A scan returns all k matching records. The requirement changes to return only whether any match exists, and records are available in a sorted query-aligned array. Which change becomes possible?

options.text: Use exact binary search for existence, removing the Ω(k) output requirement.
Continue materializing all k records because the old implementation did.
Use binary search and claim all k outputs cost O(log n).
Existence can never use binary search.

feedback.reason: Output-contract changes can alter both legality and lower bounds.
feedback.details: Binary search becomes more attractive when the result shrinks from all matches to one boolean.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.still_output_all: The new output contract is smaller.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_all_k_free: If all results were still required, output work would remain.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_only: The aligned sorted representation supports it.

items.prompt: A sorted array is tiny today but n is expected to grow by six orders of magnitude while the query contract stays exact and aligned. Which transfer principle applies?

options.text: A scan may be acceptable now, but the growing domain makes the binary-search asymptotic advantage increasingly material.
The initial scan choice must never be revisited.
Binary search is incorrect on small inputs.
The scan remains O(1) because current n is small.

feedback.reason: Transfer includes changes in the scaling regime even when correctness conditions stay constant.
feedback.details: The preferred implementation can move from simplicity-driven scanning to asymptotically superior binary search.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fixed_choice: Strategy should respond to scale changes.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_wrong_small: It remains correct; the issue is engineering preference.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_o1: n is a variable in the future workload.

items.prompt: Which changes can flip a scan decision to binary search? Select all that apply.

options.text: The data becomes query-aligned, sorted, and random-access.
A stable dataset receives enough repeated aligned queries to amortize preprocessing.
The arbitrary condition is reformulated and proven monotonic.
The problem statement adds words such as threshold and midpoint.

feedback.reason: Strategy transfer is driven by new structure or workload, not vocabulary.
feedback.details: Each accepted change supplies a missing correctness or cost precondition.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.ordered_representation: A legal efficient discard rule becomes available.

items.prompt: Which changes can flip a binary-search decision to scanning? Select all that apply.

options.text: The query predicate becomes nonmonotonic in the available order.
The result changes to first original-order match and the index lacks provenance.
The data moves to a sequential-only representation without an index.
The same stable aligned exact query is repeated more often.

feedback.reason: Binary search is not a permanent label attached to a topic.
feedback.details: Changes in query semantics, output, or access can remove its applicability or advantage.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.more_queries: This generally strengthens the case for binary search.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.original_identity: The binary result identity becomes wrong.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sequential_access: Midpoint access may lose its advantage.

items.prompt: Which dimensions should be re-evaluated when transferring the binary-search-versus-scan decision to a new problem? Select all that apply.

options.text: Order, monotonicity, and legal discard implications.
Single versus repeated queries and update frequency.
Data access model, output size, and identity requirements.
Whether familiar identifiers appear.

feedback.reason: Transfer is a structured re-derivation, not analogy from surface wording.
feedback.details: The same high-level noun can cross the decision boundary when any of these dimensions changes.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_names: Names do not transfer algorithmic guarantees.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.access_output: These affect both legality and cost.

items.prompt: Which paired scenarios differ by a decisive strategy boundary? Select all that apply.

options.text: One exact query on unsorted data versus many exact queries on a stable sorted array.
An alternating condition versus a proven false-to-true predicate.
Any key match versus first original-order match without provenance.
The same implementation with index renamed to mid.

feedback.reason: Good contrast pairs vary one decisive property while keeping the surrounding problem comparable.
feedback.details: A renaming pair is artificial because it changes no algorithmic fact.

items.prompt: Which statements should appear in a strong transferred-strategy explanation? Select all that apply.

options.text: Why both binary search and scanning initially appear plausible.
The exact condition that makes one strategy legal or cheaper.
When the rejected strategy would become appropriate.
Only the statement that one method is faster.

feedback.reason: Contrast feedback must explain the choice and its boundary, not merely name a winner.
feedback.details: The rejected strategy should remain available under explicitly changed conditions.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.faster_slogan: This omits correctness, workload, and the flip condition.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.both_plausible: The contrast should acknowledge shared surface signals.

items.prompt: Arrange the transfer analysis for a new search problem.

elements.text: Identify surface similarities to known binary-search and scan cases.
Extract order, predicate, output, access, query-count, and update constraints.
Derive legal strategies and their complete workload costs.
Choose the strategy and state the minimal change that would flip the decision.

feedback.reason: Surface analogy is only the entry point to transfer reasoning.
feedback.details: The final flip statement proves that the learner understands the boundary rather than memorizing a label.

items.prompt: Arrange the response when a formerly monotonic query gains an exception that breaks monotonicity.

elements.text: Write the new complete predicate including the exception.
Show where truth can change direction more than once.
Remove the old binary-search half-discard assumption.
Use scanning or build a representation that restores ordered structure.

feedback.reason: Transfer requires invalidating obsolete reasoning explicitly.
feedback.details: Keeping the old loop after the predicate changes is a correctness defect.

items.prompt: Arrange the response when repeated queries make preprocessing attractive.

elements.text: Estimate query and update volume over the representation lifetime.
Compare build and maintenance plus queries against repeated scans.
Adopt binary-search lookup only when the legal total workload and memory fit.

feedback.reason: Repeated-query transfer is an amortization decision with correctness constraints.
feedback.details: The index is not justified merely because future queries exist.

items.prompt: Arrange the review when the output changes from one match to all matches.

elements.text: State that every matching occurrence must be returned.
Use legal boundaries to locate the equal range when data is sorted.
Include Θ(k) work and space to emit k matches.
Compare O(log n + k) with a scan under the actual data and output contract.

feedback.reason: Binary search can still help, but the output lower bound changes the total cost.
feedback.details: Transfer should preserve the search advantage without claiming that k outputs are free.

items.prompt: A static unsorted array receives q exact queries. Select the total time of repeated scans and of sorting once followed by binary searches.

scan_time: O(n log n + q log n)
indexed_time: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n)

indexed_time.sort_then_queries: O(n log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: This formula is a reusable transfer model for static repeated exact queries.
feedback.details: The decision flips with q, legal mutation or copy cost, and practical constants.

items.prompt: A sorted array query returns all k matches. Compare boundary search plus output with a full scan plus output.
constraints: Two boundary searches cost O(log n) total.
Writing each matching result costs O(1).

feedback.reason: Output size narrows but does not erase the benefit of locating the range logarithmically.
feedback.details: When k is Θ(n), both totals become Θ(n).

items.prompt: A candidate predicate costs P(n), candidate range width is W, and a direct legal scan-style solution costs D(n). Select the answer-space binary cost and the correct transfer comparison.
constraints: The predicate is monotonic and binary search uses O(log W) evaluations.

feedback.reason: The outer logarithm is only one factor in the transferred workload.
feedback.details: A direct method remains preferable when D(n) grows more slowly or has materially lower costs.

items.prompt: A dataset has q exact queries and u updates. A maintained ordered structure costs O(log n) per query and O(log n) per update; a plain array costs O(n) per query and O(1) per append update. Select total time for both models.
constraints: The maintained structure preserves the required query semantics.
Updates are append-like in the plain array model.

feedback.reason: A different maintained representation can move both query and update costs.
feedback.details: Transfer should compare the actual data structure options rather than only sort-once versus scan.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Re-evaluate binary search versus scanning whenever query semantics, preprocessing reuse, mutation rate, access model, predicate monotonicity, or output contract changes.
